topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The dust motes danced in the single shaft of weak afternoon sun slicing through the grimy windowpane. Liam dragged a hand across his face, the skin feeling tight and hot. Every creak in the old house seemed amplified, a sharp, unwelcome sound in the heavy silence. He stared at the peeling wallpaper in the hallway, his gaze tracing patterns of decay that mirrored the landscape inside his own chest. "This is pointless," he muttered, the words barely audible above the settling silence. He shuffled toward the staircase, his footsteps sounding clumsy and heavy on the warped floorboards. He didn't want to look at the drawing-room; the sheer weight of expectation pressing down on him felt suffocating.

[story 2]

Elara sat curled on the edge of a moth-eaten armchair, hugging her knees tightly to her chest. The air in the attic room was thick with the scent of aged wood and forgotten lavender, a smell that only deepened the ache in her stomach. She picked at a loose thread on her worn sweater, her eyes fixed on a cobweb stretching across a dusty mirror. "Another burden," she whispered, her voice thin and reedy. She shifted, pulling her legs up higher, seeking a small physical barrier against the vast emptiness of the inheritance. The silence of the house wasn't peaceful; it was an accusation, loud and unwavering.

[story 3]

Marcus paced the length of the main living room, his movements jerky and agitated. He stopped abruptly by the mantelpiece, running his fingertips over the cold marble, his breath catching in shallow gasps. The sheer volume of possessions—the heavy furniture, the stacks of unopened boxes—felt like an insurmountable physical obstacle. He squeezed his eyes shut for a moment, trying to force the churning discomfort down, but it just rose again, a persistent, heavy pressure behind his ribs. "I don't know what to do with all this," he finally said, the tone flat, devoid of any real inflection. He sank onto a velvet ottoman, his shoulders slumping forward as if the weight of the house itself had physically crushed him.
